title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Maya civilization. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Maya: Architects of the Rainforest
content.text: Deep within the dense jungles of Central America lie the towering limestone pyramids of the Maya, a civilization that reached its peak while much of Europe was still in the Dark Ages. The Maya were not a single empire like the Aztecs or Incas, but a network of rival city-states sharing a common culture, religion, and sophisticated writing system. ||(41)|| [41]This agricultural success allowed for a population boom and the funding of massive public works[/41]. At the heart of their cities were the Great Plazas, surrounded by astronomical observatories and 'temple-mountains' that were designed to align with the sun during the solstices. To the Maya, architecture was a way of mapping the heavens onto the Earth. ||(42)|| [42]Their mathematical system was equally advanced, featuring the concept of zero long before it was adopted in the West[/42]. This allowed their priests to calculate celestial cycles with astonishing accuracy, creating a calendar that was more precise than the one we use today. However, this obsession with time and the stars was not just academic; it dictated every aspect of their lives, from when to plant crops to when to go to war. ||(43)|| [43]The most enduring mystery of the Maya is their sudden 'collapse' during the 9th century[/43]. One by one, the great cities of the southern lowlands were abandoned to the jungle. For decades, historians blamed a single catastrophic event, such as a massive earthquake or a foreign invasion. ||(44)|| [44]Recent core samples from lake beds suggest a period of prolonged, severe drought that lasted for decades[/44]. This environmental crisis was likely exacerbated by the Maya themselves through extensive deforestation and soil exhaustion as they struggled to feed their growing populations. ||(45)|| [45]While the great cities of the south never recovered, the Maya did not vanish[/45]. The center of civilization simply shifted north to the Yucatan Peninsula, where cities like Chichen Itza flourished for several more centuries. Here, they adapted to a drier environment, developing complex systems of underground cisterns known as 'chultunes' to store rainwater. ||(46)|| [46]Today, millions of Maya descendants still live in the region, speaking their ancestral languages and maintaining traditions that predate the Spanish conquest[/46]. The 'lost cities' are not merely ruins; they are symbols of a culture that proved remarkably resilient in the face of environmental and political upheaval.

paragraphs.A: This [41] societal complexity was built [/41] on a foundation of ingenious farming. By creating 'floating gardens' in wetlands and using slash-and-burn techniques in the forests, they produced a surplus of maize, beans, and squash that could support millions of people.
paragraphs.B: In addition to [42] these celestial alignments [/42], the buildings were often covered in intricate carvings and brightly painted murals. These artworks depicted the deeds of 'divine kings' who acted as intermediaries between the human world and the supernatural realm of the gods.
paragraphs.G: Despite [43] this high level of organization [/43], the Maya were frequently plagued by internal conflict. Rival kings competed for prestige and resources, leading to a state of near-constant warfare that may have weakened the civilization's ability to respond to external crises.
paragraphs.E: However, [44] modern archaeological evidence [/44] points to a far more complex and gradual decline. Rather than a single disaster, it appears that a combination of environmental stress and social unrest led to the eventual breakdown of the urban centers.
paragraphs.C: The [45] failure of the agricultural system [/45] during this dry spell would have led to widespread famine and the loss of faith in the divine kings, who were expected to ensure the rains through ritual bloodletting and sacrifice. The social contract that held the cities together simply dissolved.
paragraphs.F: This [46] cultural survival into the post-classic period [/46] was finally ended by the arrival of European diseases and Spanish steel. However, even then, the Maya resisted colonially for nearly two centuries, with the last independent kingdom falling as late as 1697.
paragraphs.D: Many [47] tourists are surprised to learn [/47] that the Maya pyramids were actually built without the use of metal tools or the wheel. Every stone was cut using obsidian blades and transported by human labor, a testament to the sheer organizational power of the Maya elite.

questions.explanation: A links the 'societal complexity' mentioned in the introduction to the 'agricultural success' (farming foundations) that the text discusses next.
B refers to 'these celestial alignments' (sun and solstices) and adds details about 'divine kings', which connects the architecture to the mathematical system.
G refers to the 'high level of organization' described in the previous paragraph and introduces the 'internal conflict' and 'warfare' mentioned in the text.
E contrasts the old theories of a 'single catastrophic event' with 'modern archaeological evidence' of a 'gradual decline'.
C explains how the 'failure of the agricultural system' during the drought mentioned before the gap led to social collapse and the abandonment of the cities.
F summarizes the 'cultural survival' mentioned in the Yucatan description and bridges the history to the arrival of the Spanish.
